name: pydantic-models-py
description: Create Pydantic models following the multi-model pattern with Base, Create, Update, Response, and InDB variants. Use when defining API request/response schemas, database models, or data validation in Python applications using Pydantic v2.
capabilities: Generate Base, Create, Update, Response, and InDB model variants from entity definitions
Apply camelCase JSON aliases while maintaining snake_case Python conventions
Create optional fields for PATCH/update endpoints with proper Field constraints
Add doc_type fields for Cosmos DB and similar database integrations
Produce matching TypeScript type definitions for frontend consistency
Enforce validation rules with min_length, regex patterns, and type constraints
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 21 static analysis findings are false positives. The scanner misidentified Markdown code block backticks as shell execution, and documentation references to HTTP/cryptography as actual code. SKILL.md is pure documentation with Python code examples for Pydantic model patterns. No executable code, network calls, or security risks detected.
valueStatement: Define consistent API schemas without manual boilerplate. This skill generates structured Pydantic models following established patterns for request validation, responses, and database integration.
userTitle: Build Pydantic Models with Multi-Model Pattern
limitations: Requires manual TypeScript type generation after Python model creation
Does not auto-generate database migration scripts or ORM mappings
Cannot infer business logic validation rules beyond basic type constraints
Template-based output requires manual placeholder replacement

useCases.title: REST API Schema Definition
useCases.description: Define request/response models for FastAPI or Flask endpoints with consistent validation patterns.

useCases.title: Database Document Modeling
useCases.description: Create InDB model variants with doc_type fields for Cosmos DB or MongoDB document storage.

useCases.targetUser: Full-stack teams maintaining type safety across layers
useCases.title: Frontend-Backend Contract Sync
useCases.description: Generate matching Python models and TypeScript types to ensure API contract consistency.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Model Generation
promptTemplates.prompt: Create Pydantic models for a User entity with fields: id (string), name (string, required), email (string with email validation), created_at (datetime). Use the multi-model pattern with Base, Create, Update, and Response variants.
promptTemplates.scenario: Define basic CRUD schemas for a new resource

promptTemplates.title: Model with CamelCase Aliases
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate Pydantic models for a Project entity. All fields should accept both snake_case and camelCase using Field aliases. Include workspace_id, project_name, and is_active fields.
promptTemplates.scenario: Build API models for JavaScript frontend integration

promptTemplates.title: Database Model with InDB Variant
promptTemplates.prompt: Create complete Pydantic models for a Document entity including InDB variant with doc_type field. Add min_length validation on name and optional description field for updates.
promptTemplates.scenario: Define Cosmos DB document models with proper typing

promptTemplates.title: Complex Nested Model Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Build Pydantic models for an Order entity with nested line_items array. Each line item has product_id, quantity, and price. Create full multi-model pattern with validation constraints on all fields.
promptTemplates.scenario: Model complex business entities with nested structures

bestPractices: Always separate Create and Update models - Create requires all fields, Update makes all fields optional for PATCH semantics
Use Field with explicit default=None for optional update fields to distinguish between null and unset values
Enable populate_by_name = True in Config class to accept both snake_case and camelCase from API clients
antiPatterns: Do not reuse the same model class for both request bodies and database documents - separation prevents data leakage
Avoid putting business logic in model classes - keep them as pure data schemas with validation only
Do not omit doc_type in InDB models when using Cosmos DB - queries will fail without proper type filtering

faq.question: What is the multi-model pattern and why use it?
faq.answer: The pattern creates separate model classes for different purposes: Base (shared fields), Create (required fields for POST), Update (optional fields for PATCH), Response (full output), and InDB (database document with metadata). This separation prevents accidental data exposure and enforces correct validation at each layer.

faq.question: How do camelCase aliases work with Pydantic?
faq.answer: Use Field(alias='camelCaseName') on each field and set populate_by_name = True in the Config class. This allows the model to accept both naming conventions from API clients while using snake_case internally in Python code.

faq.question: Why make all Update fields optional?
faq.answer: PATCH requests should only include fields being modified. Making all fields optional with default=None allows partial updates while distinguishing between 'field not provided' (None) and 'field set to null' (explicit null).

faq.question: What is the doc_type field for?
faq.answer: In document databases like Cosmos DB, doc_type identifies the entity type within a shared collection. The InDB model adds this field automatically so queries can filter documents by type.

faq.question: Do I need to create TypeScript types manually?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill guides you to create matching TypeScript interfaces. Consider tools like pydantic2ts or datamodel-code-generator for automatic TypeScript generation from Pydantic models.

faq.question: Can I use this pattern with SQLAlchemy or other ORMs?
faq.answer: Yes, Pydantic models work alongside ORMs. Use Pydantic for request/response validation and serialization, and ORM models for database operations. Some ORMs like SQLAlchemy 2.0 have native Pydantic integration.
